system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 1: Staff, Clefs & Musical Notes
Pages 20-25 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

    Chapter 1                                                                                         4668




    Staff, Clefs & Musical Notes
    Intervals:
    A musical interval is the distance from one note (sound) to another. They may be described
    as perfect, major, minor, diminished or augmented. The smallest existing interval is called
    half step, which can also be called half tone or semitone; two half steps form one whole step,
    which can be called whole tone.


    Musical Notes:
    Musical notes are sounds represented by determined symbols in musical notation. There
    are 12 existing notes in western music, but actually we only need 7 of them for such
    representation. We will call them natural notes: Do, Re, Mi, Fa, Sol, La, Ti. Each one of these
    notes are represented by the 7 Jirst letters from the alphabet:

      • Do = C / Re = D / Mi = E / Fa = F / Sol = G / La = A / Ti = B

    The other 5 notes will be derived from these main 7, using what we call music accidentals,
    which can rise or lower each note one half step.


    Music Accidentals:

Staff (Stave):                                                                                           4668




Staff or Stave is the set of 5 horizontal, parallel and equidistant lines which create four spaces
between themselves. Each line and each space represent a different musical pitch.
Line 5
                           Space 4
Line 4
                           Space 3
Line 3
                           Space 2
Line 2
                           Space 1
Line 1


Clefs:
The Clef is a symbol that we place on the staff to indicate in which line or space the musical
notes should be placed. The most famous and used Clef is called "Treble Clef" or also "G" Clef.
It is used for high pitched instruments such as guitar, flute, clarinet, trumpet, and soprano voice.
Notice that the curl of the Treble Clef passes through the 2nd line, which means that it will be


                  œ
the "G" note. From that note, we find all the others.


            œ œ œ

Clef also known as the "Bass Clef". Notice that there are two dots between the 4th line, which
indicates that this note is the "F" note. Therefore, as we can see the notes will change places

The three types of clefs may be subdivided into ten, and they can change position on the            4668




pentagram (staff), changing thus the reference note.

1) Treble Clef on the 1st and 2nd line of the staff.


2) C Clef on the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th and 5th line of the staff.


3) F Clef on the 3rd and 4th line of the staff.



Regardless of the many clef options, popular music usually makes use of two of them: The
Treble Clef and the Bass Clef. However, if we have an orchestra performing popular
music, everything changes and we will have to use many different clefs.

For orchestral charts, the bracket is used to show instruments belonging to the same group.          4668




Orchestral Instruments:

1) Woods: flute, piccolo, oboe, bassoon, saxophone, clarinet, harmonica and many others.
2) Brass: trumpet, trombone, tuba, horn and many others.
3) Strings: violin, viola, cello, double bass, harp, piano, guitar and many others.
